cases.eval_name: Both greeting and farewell in a complete task flow

assertions.text: The response contains both a greeting and a farewell that each include the name 'Anton'
assertions.reasoning: The response contains a greeting 'Hey Anton!' at the start and a farewell 'Bye-bye Anton, catch you next time!' at the end, both including the name 'Anton'.

assertions.text: The greeting appears before the list of TypeScript benefits
assertions.reasoning: The greeting 'Hey Anton! Let's dive in.' appears before the list of TypeScript benefits.

assertions.text: The farewell appears after the list of TypeScript benefits
assertions.reasoning: The farewell 'Bye-bye Anton, catch you next time!' appears after the list of three TypeScript benefits, which is the last content before the message ends.

assertions.text: The response lists exactly 3 benefits of TypeScript over JavaScript
assertions.reasoning: The response lists exactly 3 benefits: 1) Static Type Checking, 2) Better IDE Support, 3) Improved Maintainability.

assertions.text: The greeting and farewell use different phrasing from each other (not identical sentences)
assertions.reasoning: The greeting says 'Hey Anton! Let's dive in.' and the farewell says 'Bye-bye Anton, catch you next time!' — these are clearly different phrases from each other.

actionItems.summary: The skill achieves 100% assertion pass rate but loses on structure in the one evaluated case, suggesting the greeting/farewell framing adds minor awkwardness. The skill works functionally but the structural overhead slightly degrades output quality.
weaknesses: In eval #3, structure scored 4/5 vs baseline 5/5 — the greeting/farewell wrapper adds formatting overhead that makes responses feel less clean
Only one eval case was run, making the 'INEFFECTIVE' verdict statistically weak — delta of -0.5 from a single case is not conclusive
The skill's mandatory bookending can feel mechanical when the task itself is simple, reducing naturalness
strengths: 100% assertion pass rate — the skill reliably produces both greeting and farewell when invoked
Content quality was unaffected (5/5 in both skill and baseline) — the skill does not degrade the actual task output
Rules are clear and unambiguous, making the skill easy to follow correctly
actionItems.suggestedFocus: Remove the requirement that the greeting comes before tool calls/analysis — allow the greeting to appear in the final text response only, so it doesn't interrupt the workflow structure that evaluators score.
